subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Facebook and Congressional Regulation
stimulus:
In April of 2018, Mark Zuckerberg, the founder of the social media company Facebook, was called to testify before the Senate Committee on the Judiciary and the Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation about, among other things, a data leak that led to 87 million Facebook users' private information being sold without their consent.

Senators questioned Zuckerberg about whether Facebook could adequately regulate itself and protect personal information or whether Congress should intervene. Zuckerberg defended the company, emphasizing its mission to better connect the world. In response, Zuckerberg stated, "My position is not that there should be no regulation. I think the real question, as the Internet becomes more important in people's lives, is what is the right regulation, not whether there should be or not."

parts.prompt: Referencing the scenario, describe the enumerated power in Article I of the Constitution that gives Congress the authority to regulate a business like the one above.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must identify the Commerce Clause or interstate commerce regulation. Accept responses that name the clause and connect it to internet-based businesses crossing state lines.

scoring_points.description: Referencing the scenario, describe the enumerated power in Article I of the Constitution that gives Congress the authority to regulate a business like the one above.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the enumerated power in Article I of the Constitution that gives Congress the authority to regulate a business like the one above. Acceptable response: The Commerce Clause gives Congress the authority to regulate interstate commerce. Facebook and other internet-based companies qualify as interstate since they cross state boundary lines.

required_elements: Identifies the Commerce Clause as the enumerated power and explains its relevance to an internet company like Facebook
alternatives.correct_example: The Commerce Clause in Article I gives Congress the power to regulate interstate commerce. Because Facebook is an internet company that operates across all state lines and serves users nationwide, Congress can regulate it under this enumerated power.

wrong_examples: The Necessary and Proper Clause — this is an implied power, not an enumerated power, and the question specifically asks for an enumerated power.
The First Amendment — this protects speech from government restriction; it does not give Congress the authority to regulate businesses.
common_traps: The response must identify the Commerce Clause specifically — not just 'Article I powers'
Accept responses that explain why Facebook qualifies as interstate commerce (national user base, cross-state data flow, etc.)

sample_responses.response: The Commerce Clause gives Congress the authority to regulate interstate commerce. Facebook and other internet-based companies qualify as interstate since they cross state boundary lines.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the Commerce Clause and explains how Facebook's cross-state internet operations make it subject to congressional regulation. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Congress has the Necessary and Proper Clause which gives them power to regulate companies.
sample_responses.commentary: The Necessary and Proper Clause is an implied power, not an enumerated power as the question requires. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the two-chamber structure of the legislature affects the ability of Congress to exercise the power described in Part A.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the effect of bicameralism on the ability to pass legislation. Accept any accurate explanation of how having two chambers complicates the legislative process.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the two-chamber structure of the legislature affects the ability of Congress to exercise the power described in Part A.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the two-chamber structure of the legislature affects the ability of Congress to exercise the power described in part A. Acceptable responses include: Bicameralism makes it more difficult to pass legislation regulating commerce in Congress because legislation requires both chambers to pass the same bill; when there is divided government, meaning each chamber is led by a different political party, passing legislation can be difficult because both sides may not agree on the type of legislation that should be passed.

required_elements: Explains how bicameralism creates a more difficult path for legislation regulating commerce like Facebook
alternatives.correct_example: Because Congress has two chambers, any legislation regulating Facebook must pass both the House and the Senate in identical form. This bicameral structure makes exercising the Commerce Clause power more difficult because the same bill must survive two separate and potentially ideologically different chambers before it can become law.

required_elements: Explains how divided partisan control between chambers can further impede Commerce Clause legislation
alternatives.correct_example: If different political parties control the House and Senate, passing legislation to regulate Facebook would be even harder because majorities in both chambers must agree on the same approach, and party disagreements about government regulation of the internet could create a legislative impasse.

wrong_examples: Congress has more members so more people must agree — this is overly vague and doesn't capture the bicameralism concept.
Zuckerberg testifying before the Senate is part of the legislative process — this describes oversight, not the legislative process related to bicameralism.
common_traps: The response must explain bicameralism as a structural obstacle — not just describe how many votes are needed
Accept references to conference committees, reconciliation between chambers, or the effect of divided government

sample_responses.response: Bicameralism makes it more difficult to pass legislation regulating commerce in Congress because legislation requires both chambers to pass the same bill.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the structural obstacle of bicameralism — both chambers must agree on identical legislation. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: When there is divided government, with each chamber led by a different political party, passing legislation can be difficult because both sides may not agree on the type of regulation that should be passed.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the two-chamber structure to the complication of divided partisan control. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Congress has 535 members so it is hard to get everyone to agree.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague reference to membership size without explaining bicameralism as a structural obstacle. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: If Congress decides to regulate how social media companies gather and use the data of their users, explain how these companies could use the political process to ensure that the new regulation does not negatively affect them.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any specific and accurate political strategy that companies could use to influence the regulation. The response must describe a specific process, not just state that companies could 'fight' the regulation.

scoring_points.description: If Congress decides to regulate how social media companies gather and use the data of their users, explain how these companies could use the political process to ensure that the new regulation does not negatively affect them.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how social media companies could use the political process to ensure that the new regulation does not negatively affect them. Acceptable responses include: Social media companies might lobby members of Congress or the bureaucracy to change or interpret the law to better reflect the interests of social media companies; social media companies can make campaign contributions to candidates who support Facebook's position; social media companies could pursue litigation to challenge the regulation.

required_elements: Describes a specific political process or strategy social media companies could use to influence the regulation favorably
alternatives.correct_example: Social media companies could hire lobbyists to work with members of Congress to shape the regulation's language, ensuring it includes exceptions or provisions favorable to their business models before the bill is passed.

required_elements: Describes a specific political process or strategy
alternatives.correct_example: Social media companies could make campaign contributions through their political action committees to candidates who support light-touch regulation of the internet, hoping to elect lawmakers who will write favorable regulations.

required_elements: Describes a specific political process or strategy
alternatives.correct_example: If Congress passes unfavorable regulations, social media companies could challenge them in federal court, arguing the regulations exceed Congress's Commerce Clause authority or violate First Amendment protections for digital speech.

wrong_examples: They could stop providing services — this is not a political process.
They could tell users to contact Congress — while this is a grassroots strategy, the response must explain the mechanism more specifically.
common_traps: The response must describe a specific political process — not just state that companies 'have power' or 'could fight' the regulation
Accept lobbying, campaign contributions, litigation, public relations campaigns, or regulatory comment processes as valid strategies

sample_responses.response: Social media companies might lobby members of Congress or the bureaucracy to change or interpret the law to better reflect the interests of social media companies.
sample_responses.commentary: Names lobbying as a specific political process and explains the mechanism — influencing how the law is written or interpreted. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Social media companies could make campaign contributions to candidates who support their position on data regulation.
sample_responses.commentary: Campaign contributions to favorable candidates is a specific and accepted political process. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Social media companies would tell their users to protest the regulation.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not describe a specific political process mechanism. Telling users to protest without naming the mechanism (e.g., grassroots lobbying, petition drive) does not earn the point. 0 pts.
